title: FastText: Beyond Whole Words
concept: FastText represents each word as a bag of character n-grams, letting it generate embeddings for words not seen during training.

exercises.prompt: What happens when an older, whole-word AI model encounters a typo it has never seen before?

options.text: They automatically correct the spelling using GPS
They get completely stuck and cannot understand the word at all
They translate the word into a secret AI language

exercises.explain: Traditional models treat every word as a single entity. If they encounter a brand new word or a typo, they cannot process it because they only know whole words from their training dictionary.

cards.title: The Typo Trap
cards.body: Have you ever typed a typo like "computr" and wondered how search engines still know exactly what you meant? Old-school AI models get completely confused by misspelled or new words. Let's learn about FastText, the clever system that looks inside words to solve this!

cards.title: What is FastText?
cards.body: FastText is a smart AI tool developed by Facebook that represents words as a collection of smaller character chunks (called n-grams). Instead of learning just the word "apple", it also learns the pieces "ap", "ppl", and "le". This allows it to handle misspelled words and brand-new vocabulary easily.

cards.title: The Lego Word Builder
cards.body: Think of traditional word models like a dictionary of whole words. If a word isn't in the dictionary, the model is completely lost. FastText is like a Lego set. It breaks words down into tiny syllable-like building blocks (character n-grams). Even if it has never seen the word "unbelievable" before, it can build its meaning by putting together the blocks "un-", "believe", and "-able"!

cards.title: FastText in Action
cards.body: If FastText sees the word "eating", it doesn't just look at "eating" as a whole. It breaks it down into chunks like "<ea", "eat", "ati", "tin", "ing>", where the brackets show the start and end of the word. If it later sees the typo "eating", it still understands it because most of the chunks match!

cards.title: The Unknown Word Myth
cards.body: Many people believe that if an AI hasn't seen a word during training, it can never understand it. While this is true for older models like Word2Vec, FastText busts this myth! By looking at sub-word chunks, it can make a highly accurate guess about words it has never encountered before.

cards.title: Whole Words vs. Word Chunks
cards.body: Comparing how traditional models see words versus how FastText sees them. FastText breaks them down to find meaning in the pieces!

left.text: Sees "superfast" as one single block. If it has never seen "superfast" before, it has no idea what it means.

right.text: Sees "superfast" as "super" + "fast". It immediately understands the meaning because it knows the sub-words!

exercises.prompt: How does FastText handle words differently compared to traditional whole-word models?

options.text: It ignores all vowels in the words
It breaks words down into smaller letter chunks (n-grams)
It translates every word into French first
It only reads the first letter of each word

exercises.explain: FastText breaks words into smaller character chunks (n-grams), allowing it to analyze sub-parts of words instead of treating each word as a single indivisible unit.

exercises.prompt: True or False: FastText can generate a helpful mathematical representation (embedding) for a word it has never seen during training.
exercises.explain: This is true! Because FastText uses character n-grams, it can build representations for out-of-vocabulary ( unseen) words based on their sub-parts.

exercises.prompt: Complete the sentence to explain how FastText breaks down words.
exercises.sentence: FastText breaks words down into smaller chunks of letters called character [blank].
exercises.explain: FastText breaks words down into character n-grams, which are small overlapping sequences of letters. This helps it understand spelling variations and new words.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes a FastText-style AI successfully handling a brand-new word?

options.text: An AI that only looks at whole words and has "playful" in its vocabulary.
An AI that looks at word fragments and understands "playful" because it knows "play" and "ful".
An AI that randomly guesses the meaning of "playful" based on the first letter "p".

exercises.explain: The model that looks at word fragments can understand "playful" because it recognizes the parts "play" and "ful". The whole-word model has never seen the exact word "playful" before and fails.

exercises.prompt: Imagine a FastText AI encounters the word "subtext" for the very first time. How is it able to guess what this brand-new word means?

options.text: It looks up the word in a pre-made dictionary of slang terms
It breaks it into sub-words like 'sub' and 'text' to guess the meaning
It skips the word entirely and ignores it in the sentence
It asks the user to type in a definition for the new word

exercises.explain: FastText breaks words down into character n-grams. The word "subtext" contains sub-parts like "sub", "ubt", "bte", "tex", and "ext". By combining these parts, FastText can understand "subtext" even if it only saw "subway" and "textbook" during training!
